lint_results.output: 46 linting issues found via ruff: 40 unused imports (fixable), 3 ambiguous variable names (E741), 3 f-strings without placeholders. Run 'ruff check --fix' to auto-fix.

findings.title: Shell command construction with unquoted file paths
findings.description: In regression.py:256, the pytest coverage command is constructed with an f-string that includes an unquoted file path: f"python3 -m pytest --cov=. --cov-report=json:{cov_json} --cov-report=term". If the path contains spaces or shell metacharacters, the command will fail or have unexpected behavior.
findings.impact: Commands with paths containing spaces or special characters will break, potentially causing regression tests to fail with confusing error messages.
findings.fix_suggestion: Quote the path: use shlex.quote(cov_json) or f"python3 -m pytest --cov=. --cov-report=json:'{cov_json}' --cov-report=term"

findings.title: Subprocess shell=True usage with command injection potential
findings.description: The run_cmd function in regression.py uses subprocess.run with shell=True. While the commands come from configuration (trusted input), using shell=True increases the attack surface and makes the code more fragile. Best practice is to avoid shell=True and pass arguments as a list.
findings.evidence:
proc = subprocess.run(
    cmd,
    cwd=cwd,
    shell=True,
    capture_output=True,
    text=True,
    timeout=timeout_s,
    env={**os.environ},
)
findings.impact: Increased risk if configuration is ever loaded from untrusted sources. Current design mitigates this by using config-based commands, but the pattern is fragile.
findings.fix_suggestion: Consider redesigning to use subprocess.run with shlex.split() to parse the command string safely, or break commands into components. Document that test_command must be a valid shell command.

findings.title: 46 unused imports and code quality issues from ruff linter
findings.description: Ruff linter found 46 issues: 40 unused imports (automatically fixable), 3 ambiguous variable names (E741 - using 'l' as a variable in lambdas/comprehensions), and 3 f-strings without placeholders. These should be fixed to maintain code quality and clarity.
findings.evidence: Examples: backoffice/aggregate.py:615 imports normalize_finding but doesn't use it; tests/test_delivery.py:782 uses 'l' in lambda expression; tests/test_servers.py:48 has f-string without placeholders
findings.impact: Reduces code clarity, increases maintenance burden, and indicates incomplete refactoring in some areas.
findings.fix_suggestion: Run 'python3 -m ruff check --fix backoffice/ tests/' to automatically fix 40 issues. Manually rename 'l' to clearer names like 'lane' or 'item' to fix E741 warnings.

findings.title: CORS header echoing with wildcard allowlist
findings.description: In api_server.py:184-187, if the allowed_origins list contains '*', the code will echo back the user-provided Origin header value instead of sending just '*'. This is incorrect CORS behavior.
findings.evidence:
if "*" in allowed or origin in allowed:
    self.send_header("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", origin or "*")
findings.impact: Incorrect CORS behavior if wildcard is in allowlist. Current defaults (localhost only) are not affected, but future configuration changes could introduce this issue.
findings.fix_suggestion: Fix the logic: if "*" in allowed: self.send_header("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*") elif origin in allowed: self.send_header("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", origin)

findings.title: Large file complexity: server.py (933 lines)
findings.description: The server.py file is the largest module at 933 lines, containing DashboardServer, APIHandler, and supporting functions. Large files become harder to understand, test, and maintain.
findings.evidence: server.py is 933 lines total, containing multiple classes and concerns mixed together.
findings.impact: Harder to understand, test in isolation, and maintain. Changes to one concern may inadvertently affect another.
findings.fix_suggestion: Consider breaking into separate modules in a future refactoring: server_base.py (DashboardServer), api_handler.py (APIHandler), server_main.py (setup). This is not urgent but would improve maintainability.

findings.title: Bare exception handler in try_read_json
findings.description: In regression.py:126, a bare 'except Exception:' catches all exceptions. While intentional for robustness, it silently swallows errors that might indicate real problems like MemoryError.
findings.impact: May hide unexpected errors. Callers won't know why a file couldn't be read.
findings.fix_suggestion: Catch specific exceptions: 'except (json.JSONDecodeError, FileNotFoundError, OSError)' instead of bare Exception.

findings.title: Missing validation of Content-Length header
findings.description: In api_server.py:213, the Content-Length header is converted to int without bounds checking. A large value could cause memory issues when reading the request body.
findings.impact: Potential DoS via large Content-Length headers, though mitigated by HTTP server's built-in limits.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add bounds check: MAX_REQUEST_SIZE = 1024 * 1024; if length > MAX_REQUEST_SIZE: raise ValueError("Request too large")

findings.title: Potential inefficiency in finding normalization
findings.description: The normalize_finding function is called for every finding in the backlog merge. While correct, the code could benefit from caching if the same finding is normalized multiple times.
findings.evidence: Finding normalization is repeated in loops across multiple findings.
findings.impact: Minor performance impact on large portfolios. Not currently an issue but could become one as the system scales to thousands of findings.
findings.fix_suggestion: Monitor performance in production. If profiling shows this as a bottleneck, consider memoizing normalize_finding. Currently acceptable.

findings.title: Inconsistent path handling: mixing os.path and pathlib.Path
findings.description: Several files mix os.path operations with string paths instead of consistently using pathlib.Path. While functional, this reduces consistency with modern Python best practices.
findings.fix_suggestion: Convert to pathlib: stdout_path = Path(out_dir) / f"{label}.stdout.log"
